column1: #deck:k8s-platform-labs::01-core-concepts::03-pod-container-basics
#separator:Comma
#columns:Front
column2: Back

column1: Is a Pod restarting the same thing as a container inside it restarting?
column2: No — the Pod object itself is untouched (same IP, same UID); only the container instance is new

column1: What is the pause container's job?
column2: It's the first container kubelet creates for a Pod — it claims the network/IPC/UTS namespaces first, then your containers join them

column1: Which namespaces are shared between containers in a Pod by default, and which aren't?
column2: Network, IPC, and UTS are shared by default; PID is NOT shared by default (opt-in via shareProcessNamespace); mount is never shared; each container gets its own cgroup for accounting

column1: Why does a shared volume not mean containers see one shared filesystem?
column2: Each container still has its own separate mount namespace — a shared volume is the same volume bind-mounted separately into each container's own isolated view

column1: What does command override, and what does args override?
column2: command overrides the image's ENTRYPOINT; args overrides the image's CMD

column1: Can an admission controller change your Pod's resources.requests before scheduling?
column2: Yes — if a LimitRange exists in the namespace, the LimitRanger admission controller can inject default requests/limits before the Pod is ever scheduled

column1: Does containerPort actually open a port or configure a firewall rule?
column2: No — it's informational only. A container is reachable on any port it listens on regardless of what's declared here

column1: Is kube-proxy involved in creating a Pod's network namespace?
column2: No — kube-proxy's only job is Service routing (writing iptables/IPVS rules); it plays no role in individual pod creation or pod-to-pod networking

column1: Do CPU limits only help, with no downside?
column2: No — they prevent noisy-neighbor problems but can throttle your own container even when the node has spare CPU capacity

column1: What does READY: 0/1 mean if STATUS shows Running?
column2: The container process is running, but it's failing its readiness probe — READY and STATUS are separate signals

column1: What are the five Pod phases?
column2: Pending, Running, Succeeded, Failed, Unknown

column1: What does ImagePullBackOff mean vs CrashLoopBackOff?
column2: ImagePullBackOff = kubelet can't pull the image (registry/tag problem); CrashLoopBackOff = the container starts but keeps exiting (application problem)

column1: How is a pod's QoS class calculated?
column2: Guaranteed = requests==limits for every resource, every container. BestEffort = no requests/limits set at all. Burstable = anything in between

column1: What order does Kubernetes evict pods under node memory pressure?
column2: BestEffort first, then Burstable, then Guaranteed last

column1: Does a container run as root by default?
column2: Yes, unless securityContext (runAsUser/runAsNonRoot) says otherwise

column1: What actually happens when you run kubectl delete pod?
column2: The pod enters Terminating, kubelet sends SIGTERM, and only after terminationGracePeriodSeconds elapses without exit does kubelet send SIGKILL

column1: Why are pod IPs considered ephemeral?
column2: A recreated pod gets a new IP — this is exactly why Services exist, to provide a stable address

column1: What happens if you delete a naked pod (no controller)?
column2: Nothing recreates it — it's simply gone. Only a controller notices and recreates pods

column1: A label-selector query returns nothing. Does that mean the object doesn't exist?
column2: Not necessarily — check for a label typo before assuming nothing was created; the object could be running under an unexpected label

column1: What flag makes a one-shot debug pod delete itself automatically?
column2: --rm, combined with --restart=Never -it

column1: Do kubectl run --requests and --limits flags still work?
column2: No — both were deprecated around kubectl 1.20 and removed entirely by 1.24. Use --dry-run=client -o yaml, edit the resources block, then apply

column1: What's the exponential backoff sequence for CrashLoopBackOff, and when does it reset?
column2: 10s, 20s, 40s, 80s, 160s, capped at 5 minutes; resets to 10s after 10 consecutive minutes of the container staying up
